title: Article Mistakes (a/an/the) - 50 Mistakes
definition: Articles (a, an, the) are small words, but they cause big confusion because English decides between them based on SOUND, not spelling, and 'the' depends on whether something is specific, unique, or general. Here are 50 of the most common article mistakes, explained simply, with a clear rule to apply every time.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is a honest person.
commonMistakes.right: She is an honest person.
commonMistakes.why: The choice between 'a' and 'an' depends on the SOUND of the next word, not its first letter. 'Honest' starts with a silent 'h', so it actually begins with a vowel sound - say the word aloud and you'll hear it. Whenever you're unsure, say the word out loud: if it starts with a vowel sound, use 'an'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is an university student.
commonMistakes.right: He is a university student.
commonMistakes.why: 'University' starts with the letter 'u', but it's pronounced 'yoo-nih-VUR-sity' - and 'y' is a consonant sound. Since the sound is a consonant, it takes 'a', not 'an'. Always trust your ear over the spelling.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to become an teacher.
commonMistakes.right: I want to become a teacher.
commonMistakes.why: 'Teacher' clearly starts with a consonant sound ('t'), so it needs 'a'. This one is simple once you remember the rule is about sound: consonant sound = a, vowel sound = an.

commonMistakes.wrong: The life is beautiful.
commonMistakes.right: Life is beautiful.
commonMistakes.why: When you're making a general statement about something abstract - life, love, honesty, happiness - English usually drops the article completely. Only add 'the' when you mean one specific instance, like 'the life of a soldier'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I play a cricket every day.
commonMistakes.right: I play cricket every day.
commonMistakes.why: Names of sports and games (cricket, football, chess) don't take any article at all - this is called the 'zero article'. Just say the sport's name directly after 'play', with nothing in between.

commonMistakes.wrong: I saw an unicorn in the story.
commonMistakes.right: I saw a unicorn in the story.
commonMistakes.why: 'Unicorn' looks like it starts with a vowel, but it's pronounced 'YOO-nih-korn', starting with a consonant 'y' sound. Remember to listen for the sound, not just look at the letter.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is a MBA graduate.
commonMistakes.right: He is an MBA graduate.
commonMistakes.why: Abbreviations are pronounced letter by letter, so 'MBA' sounds like 'em-bee-ay', starting with a vowel sound. For abbreviations, say the letters out loud in your head before choosing 'a' or 'an'.

commonMistakes.wrong: She bought an new car.
commonMistakes.right: She bought a new car.
commonMistakes.why: 'New' begins with the consonant sound 'n', so it takes 'a'. This is a straightforward one - just double-check you're not automatically reaching for 'an' out of habit.

commonMistakes.wrong: The honesty is important.
commonMistakes.right: Honesty is important.
commonMistakes.why: When you're talking about a quality in general, like honesty, patience, or courage, you don't need 'the' in front of it. Save 'the' for when you mean a specific example of that quality.

commonMistakes.wrong: I go to a school by bus.
commonMistakes.right: I go to school by bus.
commonMistakes.why: When you talk about the everyday activity or purpose of a place (going to school to study, going to bed to sleep), no article is needed. If you mean the physical building itself, like 'the school near my house', then you would add 'the'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I met an one-eyed man.
commonMistakes.right: I met a one-eyed man.
commonMistakes.why: 'One' is spelled with a vowel but pronounced 'wun', starting with the consonant sound 'w'. This is one of the trickiest exceptions in English, so it's worth memorizing on its own.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is an best student in class.
commonMistakes.right: She is the best student in class.
commonMistakes.why: Superlative words - best, worst, tallest, most beautiful - describe something as number one in a group, and there can only be one 'best', so English always uses 'the' with them. Whenever you see a superlative form, pair it automatically with 'the'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Sun rises in the east.
commonMistakes.right: The sun rises in the east.
commonMistakes.why: Some things are one-of-a-kind in our world - the sun, the moon, the internet, the sky - and these unique nouns always take 'the'. Think of 'the' here as saying 'the only one there is'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I had a apple for breakfast.
commonMistakes.right: I had an apple for breakfast.
commonMistakes.why: 'Apple' clearly starts with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an'. This is one of the more obvious cases, but it's easy to slip up when speaking quickly.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is an unique opportunity.
commonMistakes.right: This is a unique opportunity.
commonMistakes.why: 'Unique' is pronounced 'yoo-NEEK', starting with the consonant 'y' sound, even though the spelling starts with a vowel letter. This follows the same pattern as 'university' and 'European' - remember the 'yoo' sound rule.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am going to a hospital to visit a friend.
commonMistakes.right: I am going to the hospital to visit a friend.
commonMistakes.why: When you're referring to a specific, known place that both speaker and listener understand (your local hospital), use 'the', even without naming it directly. Compare this to 'go to hospital' in British English, which means going as a patient in general - context changes the article.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is an unique opportunity for a beginner.
commonMistakes.right: This is a unique opportunity for a beginner.
commonMistakes.why: Again here, 'unique' begins with a consonant 'y' sound ('yoo-neek'), so it takes 'a', not 'an'. It helps to keep a short mental list of these 'yoo-' words: university, unique, European, uniform.

commonMistakes.wrong: The dogs are loyal animals.
commonMistakes.right: Dogs are loyal animals.
commonMistakes.why: When you make a general statement about an entire category using a plural noun (dogs, cats, teachers), no article is used at all. Articles are for specific or singular references, not broad generalizations.

commonMistakes.wrong: He plays the football on weekends.
commonMistakes.right: He plays football on weekends.
commonMistakes.why: As with cricket, football is a sport name and takes no article whatsoever. It can help to remember: sports = zero article, but musical instruments = 'the' (he plays the guitar).

commonMistakes.wrong: I live in an India.
commonMistakes.right: I live in India.
commonMistakes.why: Most country names are proper nouns that stand alone without any article. A few exceptions exist (the USA, the UK, the Netherlands) usually because they contain a common noun like 'states' or 'kingdom' inside the name.

commonMistakes.wrong: The Mount Everest is the tallest mountain.
commonMistakes.right: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain.
commonMistakes.why: Individual named mountains and lakes usually skip the article (Mount Everest, Lake Victoria), while mountain RANGES and groups of lakes take 'the' (the Himalayas, the Great Lakes). It's a small but important distinction to remember.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to be a astronaut.
commonMistakes.right: I want to be an astronaut.
commonMistakes.why: 'Astronaut' begins with a clear vowel sound, so it needs 'an'. Reading it aloud slowly should make this feel natural.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is an European country.
commonMistakes.right: This is a European country.
commonMistakes.why: 'European' is pronounced 'yoor-oh-PEE-an', starting with the consonant 'y' sound, just like 'university' and 'unique'. These words are spelled with a vowel but sound like they start with a consonant.

commonMistakes.wrong: She has the good sense of humor.
commonMistakes.right: She has a good sense of humor.
commonMistakes.why: When describing one quality that a person generally has, use 'a', not 'the' - 'the' would suggest there's only one possible sense of humor in the world. Use 'a' for a general trait someone possesses.

commonMistakes.wrong: The Africa has many countries.
commonMistakes.right: Africa has many countries.
commonMistakes.why: Continent names, like country names, are proper nouns that don't need an article. Simply use the name directly, with no 'the' in front.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need an advice from you.
commonMistakes.right: I need some advice from you.
commonMistakes.why: 'Advice' is an uncountable noun - you can't say 'an advice' or 'two advices' - so it never takes 'a' or 'an'. Instead, use words like 'some', 'a bit of', or 'a piece of' to talk about quantity.

commonMistakes.wrong: He gave me an information about the job.
commonMistakes.right: He gave me some information about the job.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'advice', 'information' is uncountable in English, so it can't take 'a/an' or become plural. Use 'some information' or 'a piece of information' instead.

commonMistakes.wrong: The Himalayas is a mountain in Asia.
commonMistakes.right: The Himalayas are mountains in Asia.
commonMistakes.why: Mountain ranges like the Himalayas and the Alps are treated as plural, unlike single peaks (Mount Everest), and they always take 'the'. Keep this pattern in mind: single peak = no article, range = 'the' + plural verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have a homework to do.
commonMistakes.right: I have homework to do.
commonMistakes.why: 'Homework' is an uncountable noun in English, so it never takes 'a' or a plural 's', even though it might feel like you have several separate assignments. Say 'some homework' or 'a piece of homework' if you want to show quantity.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is a honorable man.
commonMistakes.right: He is an honorable man.
commonMistakes.why: Like 'honest', the word 'honorable' has a silent 'h', so it begins with a vowel sound and needs 'an'. Whenever a word starts with 'hon-', check if the 'h' is silent.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is a easy exam.
commonMistakes.right: This is an easy exam.
commonMistakes.why: 'Easy' clearly starts with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an'. This is a simple, regular case with no hidden exceptions.

commonMistakes.wrong: I need a umbrella today.
commonMistakes.right: I need an umbrella today.
commonMistakes.why: 'Umbrella' begins with a vowel sound, so 'an' is correct here. There's no silent letter trick involved - it's simply a vowel sound from the start.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is an nurse at the hospital.
commonMistakes.right: She is a nurse at the hospital.
commonMistakes.why: 'Nurse' clearly starts with the consonant sound 'n', so it needs 'a'. This mistake often happens from typing or speaking quickly rather than a real misunderstanding of the rule.

commonMistakes.wrong: He bought a old house.
commonMistakes.right: He bought an old house.
commonMistakes.why: 'Old' begins with a vowel sound, so it takes 'an'. Practice saying pairs like 'an old house' out loud until it feels automatic.

commonMistakes.wrong: I met a honest lawyer.
commonMistakes.right: I met an honest lawyer.
commonMistakes.why: As with 'honest' earlier, the 'h' here is silent, so the word actually starts with the vowel sound 'on-est'. This is one of the most common article mistakes learners make, so it's worth memorizing this specific word.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the most beautiful place I ever saw.
commonMistakes.right: This is the most beautiful place I have ever seen.
commonMistakes.why: This isn't about articles, but about a common partner mistake: after 'ever' in this structure, English needs the present perfect tense ('have seen'), not the simple past ('saw'). Whenever you use 'the most... I ever...', follow it with 'have + past participle'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is an CEO of the company.
commonMistakes.right: He is the CEO of the company.
commonMistakes.why: A title that only one person can hold at a time within an organization (the CEO, the president, the manager) takes 'the', because it points to one specific, unique role. Use 'a/an' only if there could be more than one, like 'a manager' in a company with several managers.

commonMistakes.wrong: I want to visit a Taj Mahal.
commonMistakes.right: I want to visit the Taj Mahal.
commonMistakes.why: Famous, unique landmarks (the Taj Mahal, the Eiffel Tower, the Great Wall) are treated like one-of-a-kind objects, so they take 'the'. Think of these as 'named unique places', similar to the sun or the moon.

commonMistakes.wrong: The moon revolves around a earth.
commonMistakes.right: The moon revolves around the earth.
commonMistakes.why: 'The earth' is a unique object we all share, so like the sun and the moon, it always takes 'the'. Also note: 'earth' begins with a vowel sound, so if you ever needed 'a/an' with it, it would be 'an', never 'a'.

commonMistakes.wrong: She plays a piano beautifully.
commonMistakes.right: She plays the piano beautifully.
commonMistakes.why: When talking about playing a musical instrument, English traditionally uses 'the' (the piano, the guitar, the violin). This is different from sports, which take no article at all - instruments and sports follow opposite patterns.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is an tallest boy in class.
commonMistakes.right: He is the tallest boy in class.
commonMistakes.why: As with all superlatives, 'tallest' must be paired with 'the', since it describes the single highest-ranking person in the group. Never use 'a/an' directly before a superlative.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is a only solution.
commonMistakes.right: This is the only solution.
commonMistakes.why: 'Only' works like a superlative here - it means there is exactly one solution and no other - so it always takes 'the'. Words like 'only', 'same', and 'first' commonly pair with 'the' for this reason.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is a most talented singer I know.
commonMistakes.right: He is the most talented singer I know.
commonMistakes.why: Whenever 'most' is used to form a superlative (the most talented, the most interesting), it needs 'the' in front, not 'a'. Only use 'a/an most' when 'most' means 'very' in a different, more formal sense (a most unusual event).

commonMistakes.wrong: I need an hour to finish a homework.
commonMistakes.right: I need an hour to finish the homework.
commonMistakes.why: 'Hour' starts with a silent 'h', so it correctly takes 'an' - but 'homework' is uncountable, so if you mean a specific assignment already mentioned, use 'the homework', not 'a homework'. Two different article rules are combined in this one sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is an historical event.
commonMistakes.right: This is a historical event.
commonMistakes.why: In modern English, we pronounce the 'h' in 'historical' clearly, so it takes 'a', not 'an' (older or more formal writing sometimes used 'an historical', but this is now considered outdated). Trust modern everyday pronunciation over old-fashioned writing habits.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is going to a USA for study.
commonMistakes.right: She is going to the USA for study.
commonMistakes.why: Country names that are abbreviations, or that include a common noun like 'states', 'kingdom', or 'republic', usually take 'the' (the USA, the UK). This is different from most standalone country names like India or France, which take no article.

commonMistakes.wrong: The internet has changed a world.
commonMistakes.right: The internet has changed the world.
commonMistakes.why: 'The world' is treated as one unique, shared place, so it always takes 'the', just like the sun, the moon, and the earth. Keep a mental list of these unique, one-of-a-kind nouns that always need 'the'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is a MLA from this constituency.
commonMistakes.right: He is an MLA from this constituency.
commonMistakes.why: Abbreviations are judged by how their letters sound when spoken - 'MLA' is said as 'em-el-ay', which starts with a vowel sound. Always sound out an abbreviation letter by letter before deciding between 'a' and 'an'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I bought a SUV yesterday.
commonMistakes.right: I bought an SUV yesterday.
commonMistakes.why: 'SUV' is pronounced 'es-yoo-vee', beginning with the vowel sound 'es', so it needs 'an'. This is another abbreviation where the sound doesn't match the first letter's typical sound.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is a hour-long meeting.
commonMistakes.right: This is an hour-long meeting.
commonMistakes.why: 'Hour' has a silent 'h', so it actually begins with the vowel sound 'ow-er'. This is a classic exception worth memorizing by itself, alongside 'honest' and 'honorable'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct article: 'She is ___ honest woman.'
quiz.explanation: 'Honest' has a silent h, so it starts with a vowel sound and needs 'an'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct article: 'He is ___ university professor.'
quiz.explanation: 'University' starts with a 'yoo' consonant sound, so it needs 'a'.

quiz.sentence: I need an advice from you.
quiz.correction: I need some advice from you.
quiz.explanation: 'Advice' is uncountable and never takes 'a/an'; use 'some advice' instead.

quiz.question: ___ sun rises in the east. (unique object)
quiz.explanation: Unique objects like the sun always take 'the'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: Sports and games take zero article - no 'a/an/the' at all.
